216	\begin{lemma}[Cartesian-monoidal collapse identity]
217	  \label{lem:rigidity_snd_lift}
218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_snd_lift}
219	  Let \(X\), \(Y\) be objects over \(\bar k\) and fix a \(\bar k\)-point \(x_0\) of \(X\).
220	  Post-composing the second projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) with the slice section
221	  \(y \mapsto (x_0, y)\) equals the ``collapse the \(X\)-coordinate onto \(x_0\)'' endomorphism
222	  \((x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\) of \(X \times Y\). This is pure cartesian-monoidal algebra (no
223	  geometry): the product universal property distributes \(p_2\), the \(Y\)-component simplifies by
224	  the identity law, and the \(X\)-component collapses by uniqueness of maps to the terminal object.
225	\end{lemma}
226	\begin{lemma}[Bridge~1: completeness makes the projection a closed map]
227	  \label{lem:snd_left_isClosedMap}
228	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.snd_left_isClosedMap}
229	  \uses{lem:projectiveLineBar_isProper}
230	  Let \(X\) be complete (proper) over \(\bar k\) and \(Y\) any object over \(\bar k\). Then the
231	  underlying topological map of the second projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a
232	  \emph{closed} map. This is Mumford's ``completeness of \(X\) makes \(p_2\) a closed map'': the
233	  projection is the base change of \(X \to \Spec \bar k\) along \(Y \to \Spec \bar k\), properness
234	  of \(X\) gives universal closedness, and universal closedness is stable under base change, so the
235	  base-changed projection is universally closed and hence a closed map. Characteristic-free; no
236	  theorem of the cube, no cohomology.
237	\end{lemma}
238	\begin{lemma}[Scheme-level gluing core of the Rigidity Lemma]
239	  \label{lem:rigidity_core}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_core}
241	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
242	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed, \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible,
243	  reduced and locally of finite type over \(\bar k\), and \(Z\) separated. Let
244	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) collapse the slice \(X \times \{y_0\}\) to a single point \(z_0\).
245	  Then \(f\) equals its composite with the collapse endomorphism \((x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\), i.e.\
246	  \(f = \mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\). The proof takes the non-empty open \(U\) on which \(f\) and
247	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}); since \(X \times Y\)
248	  is geometrically irreducible over the one-point base \(\Spec \bar k\), it is irreducible, so the
249	  non-empty open \(U\) is dense and its inclusion is dominant; the dominant-source/separated-target
250	  rigidity handle then promotes agreement on \(U\) to equality of the two morphisms everywhere.
251	\end{lemma}
252	\begin{lemma}
253	\leanok
254	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
255	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
257	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine, lem:snd_left_isClosedMap}
258	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
259	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
260	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
261	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
262	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
263	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
264	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
265	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
266	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety and \(Y\), \(Z\) any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
267	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a point \(x_0 \in X\), and suppose given
268	  \(y_0 \in Y\), \(z_0 \in Z\) with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
269	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
270	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
271	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
272	  Then there is a non-empty open subset \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) on which \(f\) agrees, as a
273	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\), where
274	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
275	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)\( is the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
276	  \(U = X \times V\) with \(V := Y - G\), \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))\) for an affine open
277	  neighbourhood \(U_0\) of \(z_0\), and on \(U\) one has \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)\).
278	  \medskip
279	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of \(V\) is exactly
280	  the assertion \(y_0 \notin G\), and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
281	  \subseteq U_0\( keeps the slice over \)y_0\( inside the affine \)U_0\(, away from \)F = Z - U_0$. A
282	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for \(X = Y = Z\) and
283	  \(f = p_1\) the first projection, \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0\)
284	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in \(\{x = x_0\}\), which has empty
285	  interior in the irreducible \(X \times Y\)). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
286	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
287	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
288	  \medskip
289	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
290	  source.} The prose already takes \(\bar k\) algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
291	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
292	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field \(\kappa(y)\) to be \(\bar k\) itself, so that a
293	  proper integral slice has global sections \(\Gamma = \bar k\) and maps to a single \(\bar k\)-point of
294	  the affine \(U_0\); this requires \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\). Second, the globalisation of the
295	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
296	  closed points of the saturated open \(U\) to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ \(U\) to be a Jacobson space.
297	  Over the algebraically closed \(\bar k\) this holds precisely because \(X \times Y\) is \emph{locally of
298	  finite type} over \(\bar k\) (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
299	  property of \(\Spec \bar k\), and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
300	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\). Accordingly the formalization carries
301	  \emph{both} \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and
302	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\) on this lemma (in addition to
303	  \([\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]\)), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
304	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
305	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
306	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
307	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
308	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
309	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) --- the base-field variable is literally named \(\mathtt{kbar}\) ---
310	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over \(\bar k\), so the
311	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
312	\end{lemma}

314	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
315	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
316	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
317	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
318	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
319	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
320	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for

323	\begin{proof}
324	  \leanok
325	  Let \(U_0\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U_0\), and set
326	  \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a
327	  \emph{closed} map, so \(G\) (the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\)) is closed in \(Y\). The
328	  collapse hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0\) gives
329	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), hence \(y_0 \notin G\); this is the only place
330	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for \(V := Y - G\) is non-empty precisely
331	  because \(y_0 \in V\). For each \(y \in V\) the proper variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is mapped by \(f\)
332	  into the affine \(U_0\), hence to a single point, necessarily \(f(x_0, y)\). So for all \(x \in X\),
333	  \(y \in V\), we have \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)\), i.e.\ \(f\) and
334	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on the non-empty open \(U := X \times V\). (In the Lean
335	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
336	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
337	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
338	  \medskip
339	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
340	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
341	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
342	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
343	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``\(X\) complete \(\Rightarrow p_2\) closed'' is
344	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
345	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on \(X\), base-changed across the canonical pullback square
346	  to make \(p_2\) universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
347	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites \((\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}\) to
348	  \(\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}\) exactly. This step is already
349	  proven.
350	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of \(V\)) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
351	  \(X \times \{y_0\}\) over the \(\bar k\)-rational point \(y_0\) is exactly the image of the slice
352	  section \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
353	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
354	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
355	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
356	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1\) and
357	  \(s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0\) come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
358	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
359	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
360	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
361	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]\).
362	  \emph{Bridge~2 (slice-constancy / the agreement equation) --- RESOLVED route, cohomology-FREE.}
363	  ``Proper connected slice into affine \(\Rightarrow\) single point, glued to a morphism equality on
364	  \(U\)'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
365	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) statement: the
366	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no \(H^0\) base change), and route~(c)
367	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
368	  stack is: for each closed point \(y \in V\) one has \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\) (alg-closed, finite type);
369	  the slice \(X_y\) is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
370	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over \(\bar k\)
371	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite
372	  extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring
373	  map on global sections (\texttt{ext\_of\_isAffine}), so \(f\) and
374	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
375	  locally-of-finite-type \(U\) (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of
376	  locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-schemes; in the formalization this density is supplied by the new
377	  hypothesis \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)), so the per-slice
378	  agreement globalises to all of \(U\) via the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
379	  \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (the same handle \texttt{rigidity\_core} already
380	  uses). The packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal''
381	  hom-extensionality --- the one connective Mathlib does not supply directly (it gives only the
382	  single-dominant-morphism \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}) --- has now been built as the axiom-clean
383	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2): it assembles the closed points into one
384	  dominant coproduct probe and applies \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}. The whole argument is extracted
385	  into the dedicated obligation \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} below, whose body
386	  wires that proven Step~2 over the per-slice Step~1
387	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice
388	  constancy) is now the chain's \emph{single} genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
389	\end{proof}
390	\begin{lemma}
391	\leanok
392	[Bridge~2: slice-constancy on a saturated open mapping into an affine]
393	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
394	
395	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
396	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
397	  % NOTE (iter-161): the iter-160 SIGNATURE GAP is RESOLVED. The Lean proof wires Step 2
398	  % (morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, PROVEN axiom-clean) over Step 1
399	  % (rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, the residual sorry). Step 2 needs the closed
400	  % points of U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson; over k̄ this follows from (X⊗Y) being locally of finite
401	  % type, so [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom] is now a stated hypothesis of this lemma (and across the
402	  % chain). With it, the `JacobsonSpace U` instance is a routine discharge (LocallyOfFiniteType.
403	  % jacobsonSpace + JacobsonSpace.of_isOpenEmbedding), no longer an as-typed gap. The only `sorry`
404	  % reachable from this lemma is now Step 1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy). The hypothesis
405	  % set is recorded in the statement and proof prose below (both [IsAlgClosed k̄] AND
406	  % [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom]); the earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim
407	  % is retired.
408	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
409	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
410	  % \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
411	  % any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
412	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
413	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
414	  ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
415	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) be objects of
416	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) with \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced,
417	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over \(\bar k\) (the formalization carries
418	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\), which makes the saturated open \(U\)
419	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
420	  consumes), and \(Z\) separated. Let \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a \(\bar k\)-point
421	  \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
422	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\( for the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
423	  endomorphism. Suppose:
424	  \begin{itemize}
425	    \item \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) is a \(p_2\)-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
426	      for a set \(V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y\) (so \(U\) contains the whole fibre \(X_y\) over each of
427	      its points);
428	    \item \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) is an \emph{affine} open;
429	    \item \(f(U) \subseteq U_0\), i.e.\ \(f\) maps \(U\) into the affine \(U_0\).
430	  \end{itemize}
431	  Then \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on \(U\) as scheme morphisms:
432	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\).
433	\end{lemma}
434	\begin{proof}
435	  \leanok
436	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete
437	  variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single
438	  point of \(U\)''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
439	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
440	  \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
441	  \(H^0\) base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
442	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.
443	  \medskip
444	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
445	  Fix a closed point \(x\) of \(U\), lying over a closed point \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\).
446	  Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type over \(\bar k\), the residue field
447	  is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Because \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) is saturated, \(U\) contains the
448	  \emph{entire} fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\); hence \(f\) maps the proper integral slice \(X_y\) into the
449	  affine \(U_0\). By \(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\) the global sections \(\Gamma(X_y)\) form a
450	  field, module-finite over \(\bar k\) by \(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\), and
451	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into
452	  the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring map on global sections (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), so the
453	  restriction of \(f\) to \(X_y\) factors through a single \(\bar k\)-point of \(U_0\) --- necessarily the
454	  point \(f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)\). Thus \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\)
455	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of \(U\). This per-slice statement is the
456	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
457	  \medskip
458	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
459	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-scheme \(U\)
460	  (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\) / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
461	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)). To turn ``\(f\) and
462	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on \(U\),
463	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
464	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
465	  \(\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U\), whose range is dense --- and feeds
466	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (the same
467	  family \(\mathtt{rigidity\_core}\) uses). This yields the morphism equality
468	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\) on all of \(U\). The
469	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
470	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
471	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\)); that bespoke build is now done in
472	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
473	\end{proof}
474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
475	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
476	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
477	  
478	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
479	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
480	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
481	  Let \(W\), \(Z\) be schemes with \(W\) \emph{reduced}, \(W\) a \emph{Jacobson space}, and \(Z\)
482	  \emph{separated}. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z\) be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
483	  \(x \in W\), the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
484	  \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to W\) (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
485	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\(), then \)g_1 = g_2$.
486	\end{lemma}
487	\begin{proof}
488	\leanok
489	  This is the ``dense closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
490	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
491	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
492	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
493	  dominant probe.
494	  Form the coproduct \(P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)\) and the morphism
495	  \(\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W\) defined by \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\) of the residue-field points
496	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\). Its topological range contains every closed point of \(W\):
497	  the inclusion \(\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P\) composes with \(\mathrm{probe}\) to
498	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) (the defining property of \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\)), and the
499	  range of \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) is the singleton \(\{x\}\)
500	  (\(\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}\)). Because \(W\) is a Jacobson space, the closed points
501	  are dense (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\), i.e.\ \(\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W\)), so
502	  the range of \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dense and \(\mathrm{probe}\) is \emph{dominant}.
503	  Componentwise, \(g_1\) and \(g_2\) agree after \(\mathrm{probe}\): precomposing with each coproduct
504	  inclusion \(\iota_x\) reduces (by \(\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}\)) to
505	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\),
506	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point \(x\). By the universal property of the coproduct
507	  (\(\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}\)) this gives \(\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2\).
508	  Since \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dominant and \(Z\) is separated, \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) cancels it
509	  on the left to yield \(g_1 = g_2\).
510	\end{proof}
511	\begin{lemma}
512	\leanok
513	[Global-sections constancy: two \(\bar k\)-points of a proper integral source into an affine agree]
514	  \label{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
516	  % Project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas: NO external source. Realises, cohomology-free, the
517	  % classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over an algebraically closed
518	  % field is constant" --- precisely the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma proof quoted
519	  % above (the "complete slice into an affine maps to a single point" sentence, Ch.~II \S4, p.~43).
520	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(W\) be an integral scheme equipped with a structure
521	  morphism \(w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k\) that is universally closed (proper) and locally of finite
522	  type. Let \(g \colon W \to V\) be a morphism into an \emph{affine} scheme \(V\). Then any two
523	  \(\bar k\)-points of \(W\) --- i.e.\ any two sections \(a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W\) of \(w\) (so
524	  \(a \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\) and \(b \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\)) --- satisfy \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\).
525	  \medskip
526	  \noindent\textbf{Every hypothesis is load-bearing} (confirmed by the iter-161 audit).
527	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) collapses the finite extension \(\Gamma(W)/\bar k\); integrality of
528	  \(W\) makes \(\Gamma(W)\) a domain (without it a two-point disjoint union is a counterexample);
529	  \(\mathtt{UniversallyClosed}\) is what upgrades \(\Gamma(W)\) to a \emph{field} (without it
530	  \(\mathbb A^1\), with \(\Gamma = \bar k[t]\) and two distinct \(\bar k\)-points, is a counterexample);
531	  \(\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\) gives finiteness of \(\Gamma(W)\) over \(\bar k\); and
532	  \([\mathtt{IsAffine}\ V]\) is what lets \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\) pin the morphism by its ring map
533	  on global sections.
534	\end{lemma}
535	\begin{proof}
536	\leanok
537	  Because \(W\) is integral and \(w\) is universally closed, the global sections
538	  \(\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)\) form a field (\(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\)), and the structure
539	  ring map \(w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)\) (the map \(\mathtt{wk.appTop}\) after the canonical
540	  identification \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k) \cong \bar k\)) is integral / module-finite
541	  (\(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\)). Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and
542	  \(\Gamma(W)\) is a domain finite over it, that map is bijective
543	  (\(\mathtt{IsAlgClosed.ringHom\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}\)); thus \(\Gamma(W) = \bar k\) and
544	  \(w^\sharp\) is an isomorphism on global sections --- in particular an epimorphism. Both sections
545	  \(a\), \(b\) left-invert \(w^\sharp\) on global sections (they are sections of \(w\)), so they induce the
546	  \emph{same} ring map \(a^\sharp = b^\sharp\) on \(\Gamma(W)\) (cancel the epimorphism \(w^\sharp\)). A
547	  morphism into the \emph{affine} \(V\) is pinned by its global-sections ring map
548	  (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), whence \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\). This realises, without
549	  cohomology, the classical ``proper connected variety into an affine is a single point'' step
550	  quoted from Mumford above.
551	\end{proof}

9	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
10	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
11	
12	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
13	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
14	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
15	\[
16	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
17	  \qquad
18	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
19	\]
20	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
21	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
22	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
23	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
24	
25	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
26	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
27	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
28	% groups)
29	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
30	% SOURCE QUOTE:
31	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
32	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
33	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
34	% abelian groups defined by the formula
35	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
36	% \end{dfn}
37	%  [\dots]
38	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
39	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
40	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
41	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
42	% topologies  by
43	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
44	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
45	%  \end{dfn}"
46	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
47	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
48	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
49	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
50	homomorphisms.}
51	
52	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
53	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
54	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
55	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
56	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
57	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
58	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
59	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
60	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
61	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
62	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
63	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
64	structure. This is the content of
65	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
66	
67	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
68	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
69	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
70	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
71	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
72	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
73	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
74	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
75	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
76	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
77	\begin{enumerate}
78	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
79	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
80	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
81	    isomorphism classes;
82	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
83	    for \(\otimes\);
84	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
85	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
86	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
87	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
88	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
89	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
90	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
91	\end{enumerate}
92	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
93	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
94	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
95	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
96	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
97	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
98	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
99	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
100	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
101	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
102	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
103	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
104	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
105	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
106	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
107	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
108	supplied.
109	
110	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
111	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
112	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
113	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
114	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
115	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
116	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
117	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
118	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
119	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
120	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
121	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
122	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
123	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
124	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
125	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
126	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
127	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
128	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
129	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
130	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
131	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
132	avoids. The
133	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
134	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
135	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
136	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
137	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
138	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
139	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
140	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
141	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
142	
143	\section{Mathlib API survey}
144	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(k\) in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus \(g = g(C)\).
4	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of \(C\) in the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety even when \(C(k) = \emptyset\) (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\) and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus \(0\) the witness object is instead the trivial scheme \(\Spec k\), \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma \(+\) additivity Corollary~1.5 \(+\) Corollary~1.2 \(+\) rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\), is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-\(0\) witness consumes.
5	\section{The Albanese construction}
6	For a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\), the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from \(C\). Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
7	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
8	Let \(C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)\) be the \(g\)\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of \(C\) (the quotient of \(C^g\) by the action of the symmetric group \(S_g\)). It is a smooth proper scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
9	\[
10	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
11	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
12	\]
13	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing \(P_0\) gives a translation isomorphism \(\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}\), and the Stein factorisation of \(\sigma\) yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
14	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of \(C\) inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
15	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
16	\begin{definition}\leanok
17	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
18	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
19	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
20	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
21	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
22	  % such \(b\) arises from a unique map \(a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}\) such that
23	  % \(a(0)=0\).  Since \(B^*\) is integral, \(a\) factors through \(P\).  Thus the
24	  % maps \(a\:B^*\to P\) and \(b\:X\to B\) are in bijective correspondence.
25	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps \(b'\:B\to B'\) such
26	  % that \(b'(0)=0\).  In particular, \(1_P\) corresponds to a natural map
27	  % \(u\:X\to A\) such that \(u(x)=0\), and every map \(b\:X\to B\) factors
28	  % uniquely through \(u\)."
29	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
30	  Let \((C, P)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with a marked \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\). A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) is an \emph{Albanese object} for \((C, P)\) if there exists a morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) sending \(P\) to the identity element \(\eta \in J\), and such that every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\) factors uniquely as \(f = \iota \circ g\) for some morphism of group schemes \(g \colon J \to A\).
31	\end{definition}
32	\begin{remark}
33	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
34	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on \(J\) --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on \(\iota\) and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on \(J\) sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on \(\Jac(C)\) before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety \(A\) in the universal-property clause.
35	\end{remark}
36	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
37	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term \(h\) packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition \(P \circ \iota = \eta_J\) and the universal-factorisation property of \(\iota\). The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
38	\begin{definition}
39	\leanok
40	[The Albanese universal morphism]
41	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
42	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
43	
44	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
45	  Given a pointed curve \((C, P)\) and an Albanese term \(h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J\), the \emph{universal morphism} of \(h\) is the morphism
46	  \[
47	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
48	  \]
49	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of \(h\).
50	\end{definition}
51	\begin{lemma}
52	\leanok
53	[Pointed condition for the universal morphism]
54	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}
55	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
56	
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.comp_ofCurve}
58	  The universal morphism \(h.\mathtt{ofCurve}\) sends the marked point \(P\) to the identity of \(J\):
59	  \[
60	    P \circ h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \;=\; \eta_J.
61	  \]
62	\end{lemma}
63	\begin{lemma}
64	\leanok
65	[Universal factorisation through the Albanese object]
66	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
67	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
68	
69	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
70	  For every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) over \(k\) with \(P \circ f = \eta_A\), there exists a unique morphism \(g \colon J \to A\) such that \(f = h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \circ g\).
71	\end{lemma}
72	This extraction API is precisely what the protected interface \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} consumes: each protected wrapper takes a marked point \(P \in C(k)\) as input, projects the per-point Albanese data from the bundled witness \texttt{JacobianWitness} (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) at that \(P\), and applies the corresponding helper above.
73	\begin{theorem}\leanok
74	[Uniqueness of the Albanese object]
75	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
76	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}
78	  Let \(J_1, J_2\) be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve \((C, P)\), with universal pointed morphisms \(\iota_1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1\) and \(\iota_2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2\) (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}). Then there is a unique morphism \(e \colon J_1 \to J_2\) satisfying \(\iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e\). The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation \(\mathrm{id}\), and the produced morphism \(e\) is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of \(e\) as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark~\ref{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}).
79	\end{theorem}
80	\begin{remark}
81	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}
82	  The Lean proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique} internally produces an inverse morphism \(h \colon J_2 \to J_1\) and verifies \(g \circ h = \mathrm{id}_{J_1}\) and \(h \circ g = \mathrm{id}_{J_2}\) (the invertibility witnesses are computed as intermediate lemmas of the tactic block) before returning the triple \((g,\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ g,\ \text{uniqueness})\). The invertibility witnesses are computed but not retained in the return type
83	  \[
84	    \exists! (e : J_1 \to J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.
85	  \]
86	  The natural strengthening would be to return
87	  \[
88	    \exists! (e : J_1 \cong J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.\mathtt{hom},
89	  \]
90	  packaging the produced morphism together with its inverse. Tightening the conclusion in this way is a Lean-side refactor of the signature of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} that the project may carry out in a future iteration. The downstream consumers of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} (the protected \texttt{AbelJacobi.Jacobian.*} interface) use only the morphism-and-uniqueness content of the conclusion, so the current weaker conclusion is sufficient for the present API.
91	\end{remark}
92	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
93	\begin{definition}\leanok
94	[Jacobian]
95	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
96	  \label{def:Jacobian}
97	  
98	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
99	  The \emph{Jacobian} of \(C\) is the abelian variety \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) obtained as the Albanese object of \(C\): a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g(C)\) equipped with a universal morphism \(C \to \Jac(C)\) (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\). Formally, \(\Jac(C)\) is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-\(P\)) Albanese witness for \(C\) provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
100	  When \(g(C) = 0\) the Jacobian collapses to \(\Spec k\) itself, which is the terminal object in the category of \(k\)-schemes. When \(g(C) > 0\) the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers \(C\) itself (after choosing a \(k\)-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: \(\Jac(C)\) is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-\(0\) specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over \(k\) of relative dimension \(0\) is isomorphic to \(\Spec k\).
101	\end{definition}
102	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
103	The four protected instances on \(\Jac(C)\) are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.
104	\begin{theorem}\leanok
105	[Group object structure]
106	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
107	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
108	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
109	  \(\Jac(C)\) carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
110	\end{theorem}
111	\begin{proof}
112	  By Definition~\ref{def:Jacobian}, \(\Jac(C) = (\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C).J\) as a \(k\)-scheme, where \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\) is the Albanese witness extracted from Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} via \texttt{Classical.choice}. The witness carries the group-object structure as its field \(\mathtt{grpObj} : \mathrm{GrpObj}\, J\), so \(\Jac(C)\) inherits it by projection.
113	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if \(\iota \colon C \to J\) is the universal morphism sending the marked point \(P\) to the identity, then the diagonal \(C \times C \to J \times J\), post-composed with the universal morphism out of \(C \times C\) (factored through \(J\) by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. Uniqueness of the resulting group-object structure is again the universal-property argument of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}.
114	\end{proof}

137	  \(\Jac(C) \to \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\).
138	\end{theorem}
139	\begin{proof}
140	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{smoothGenus} : \mathrm{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,(\genus\, C)\,J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\); the relative dimension \(g(C)\) is recorded directly in the witness so that no separate dimension calculation is needed on the Lean side.
141	  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of \(C\) equals the genus \(g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to \(\Jac(C)\) at the identity is canonically identified with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of \(\mathcal O_C\) is classified by an element of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\), and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\). Smoothness of \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any \(k\)-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of \(\Jac(C)\) at every \(k\)-rational point is the same as at the identity.
142	\end{proof}

160	\begin{proof}

162	  Mathematically, properness of the Albanese variety is part of its construction. In the Picard-scheme route, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a closed subgroup scheme of the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), which is itself proper over \(\Spec k\) because \(C\) is proper and geometrically connected (FGA representability for \(\Pic\) of a smooth proper geometrically connected curve produces a proper group scheme; cf.\ FGA Explained, Chapter~9). In the symmetric-power route, properness of \(\Pic^g_{C/k}\) (and hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) after translation) follows from properness of \(\Sym^g(C)\) together with the universal closedness of the surjection \(\sigma\). In either case, properness descends to \(\Jac(C)\).

186	\begin{proof}

188	  Mathematically, the Albanese variety \(\Jac(C)\) is the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (the Picard scheme), and the identity component of a group scheme of finite type over a field is geometrically irreducible (in fact, geometrically connected, and since it is smooth at the identity by Theorem~\ref{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus} it is geometrically irreducible). Equivalently, in the symmetric-power route, \(\Jac(C)\) arises as the Stein factorisation of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) followed by a translation; \(\Sym^g(C)\) is geometrically irreducible (being the quotient of \(C^g\), itself geometrically irreducible as a product of geometrically irreducible factors, by a finite group action), so its image under the Stein factorisation is geometrically irreducible as well.

190	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
191	\begin{itemize}
192	  \item \(g = 0\): Then \(\Jac(C) = \Spec k\), the trivial group scheme; \(\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(0 = g\).
193	  \item \(g = 1\): Then \(\Jac(C) \cong C\) once a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\) is fixed; \(C\) is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension \(1\)).
194	  \item \(g \ge 2\): \(\Jac(C)\) is a non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to \(C\) as a scheme.
195	\end{itemize}
196	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
197	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: \(\Jac(C)\) is the connected component of the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). An equivalent route, available whenever a \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\) has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) and translate by \(-gP\).
198	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
199	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of \(C\). This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.
200	\begin{definition}
201	\leanok
202	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
203	[Albanese witness for a curve]
204	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}
205	  
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.JacobianWitness}
207	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) over \(k\) is a bundle of the following seven fields:
208	  \begin{itemize}
209	    \item \(J\): an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\) --- the candidate Albanese object.
210	    \item \(\mathtt{grpObj}\): a group-object structure on \(J\) in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
211	    \item \(\mathtt{proper}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is proper.
212	    \item \(\mathtt{smooth}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth.
213	    \item \(\mathtt{geomIrred}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is geometrically irreducible.
214	    \item \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\). This refines \(\mathtt{smooth}\) at the specific relative dimension \(\genus\, C\) (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
215	    \item \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\): a proof that, for every \(k\)-rational marked point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
216	  \end{itemize}
217	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
218	\end{definition}
219	\begin{remark}
220	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
221	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
222	  \[
223	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
224	  \]
225	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single \(J\) is chosen once (intrinsic to \(C\)), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
226	  \[
227	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
228	  \]
229	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to \(C\): a different choice of \(P \in C(k)\) alters the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism of \(J\), and so leaves \(J\) unchanged as a scheme. The reversed bundling is what makes the per-\(P\) projection in Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} clean: \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), and \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) each take a marked point \(P\) as input and project the corresponding component of \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P\) via the extraction trio (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}, Lemmas~\ref{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}, \ref{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}).
230	\end{remark}
231	\begin{remark}
232	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}
233	  The fields \(\mathtt{smooth}\) and \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) of an Albanese witness are mathematically redundant: the relative-dimension version implies the unqualified version via Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothOfRelativeDimension.smooth}. Keeping both fields is a Lean-level convenience: \(\mathtt{smooth}\) is the form required by the typeclass binder of \texttt{IsAlbanese} (and by the protected \(\mathtt{Jacobian.instGrpObj}\) projection), whereas \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) is the form required by the protected \(\mathtt{Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) projection. The redundancy carries no mathematical cost (the two fields agree) and no soundness cost (deriving one from the other would only re-run the same Mathlib instance synthesis at the projection sites).
234	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, def:JacobianWitness}
235	\end{remark}
236	\begin{theorem}\leanok
237	[Existence of an Albanese variety uniformly over the marked point]
238	  \label{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
239	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, def:genusZeroWitness, def:positiveGenusWitness}
240	
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.nonempty_jacobianWitness}
242	  % Cycle-fix (iter-149 plan-agent): drop the spurious 
243	  % from this statement-block. Mathematically `def:Jacobian` is the entity
244	  % that depends on this existence theorem (def:Jacobian's  lists
245	  % thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness), not the other way around. Listing
246	  % def:Jacobian here as a hypothesis of the existence theorem introduced

248	  % that crashes `leanblueprint web` with RecursionError in depgraph.ancestors.
249	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Exercise ex:jac (generalized Jacobians of a family of curves form a smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\), projective iff \(X/S\) smooth); arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
250	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers
251	  % are integral curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.
252	  % Show the ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
253	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
254	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth."
255	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Exercise (ex:jac).}
256	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field \(k\). There exists a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) of relative dimension \(g(C)\) such that, for every \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) is an Albanese object for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}. Equivalently, the type of Albanese witnesses for \(C\) (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) is non-empty.
257	\end{theorem}
258	\begin{proof}
259	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-\(0\) sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\) --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from \(\bar k\) to the base field \(k\) along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\)). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme \(J\); only the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) varies with the marked point \(P\). We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
260	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a \(P\)-independent witness.}
261	  The witness \(J\) depends only on \(C\), not on the marked point \(P\). Indeed, both classical constructions below produce \(J\) as an intrinsic object of \(C\) (the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\), or the Stein-factorisation target of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) translated by a fixed \(P_0\)); a different choice of \(P\) in \(C(k)\) alters \(\iota_P\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism, and so does not change \(J\) as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling \(J\) as a single field and the universal-property data as \(\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J\). We now turn to producing \(J\).
262	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
263	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
264	  \begin{itemize}
265	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a \(k\)-scheme \(T\), write \(C_T := C \times_k T\) and let \(\pi \colon C_T \to T\) be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
266	    \[
267	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
268	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
269	    \]
270	    A \(T\)-point is a line bundle on \(C_T\) modulo a line bundle pulled back from \(T\). The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C_T)\) descends to the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) and gives \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) a natural group-functor refinement.
271	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For \(C\) smooth proper geometrically connected over \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point, \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) (after \'etale sheafification, if \(k\) is not algebraically closed) is representable by a \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k\), which is needed for the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) to be \'etale-locally injective.
272	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) be the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\). As the identity component of a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; for \(C\) smooth proper of genus \(g\), it is moreover a smooth proper \(k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\). Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\) is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the kernel of the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\), which is locally constant). Take \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
273	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each \(P \in C(k)\), the Abel--Jacobi morphism
274	    \[
275	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
276	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
277	    \]
278	    sends \(P\) to the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). It is universal among morphisms \(f \colon C \to A\) to smooth proper geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group schemes \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\): this is the classical Albanese property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), proved by reducing to the case \(k = \overline k\) (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on \(C \times A\) trivialised along \(\{P\} \times A\) correspond bijectively to morphisms \(C \to A^{\vee} = A\) sending \(P\) to \(\eta_A\).
279	  \end{itemize}
280	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route A.} Each sub-step is missing.
281	  \begin{itemize}
282	    \item Sub-step A.1 requires a working theory of relative line bundles on a product \(C \times_k T\) and the descent of the abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\) along \(\pi^*\). The pieces are present individually but not assembled. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 700\)--\(1100\) LOC, \(\sim 6\)--\(10\) iters.)
283	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\), which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels). The construction engine for this Quot/Hilbert layer is Nitsure, ``Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes'' (FGA Explained; arXiv:math/0504590), \S\,4 (generic flatness and the flattening stratification) and \S\,5 (the construction of \(\mathrm{Quot}\), hence \(\mathrm{Hilb}\), as a scheme); see \texttt{references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md}. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2200\)--\(3000\) LOC, \(\sim 15\)--\(25\) iters --- the dominant Route A block.)
284	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction \(G^0 \subseteq G\), and the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\). (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 600\)--\(900\) LOC, \(\sim 5\)--\(8\) iters.)
285	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form. \textbf{Bypass via Milne autoduality FAILS}: as Milne III \S~6 writes the proof of Proposition~6.1, it invokes Theorem~3.2 (rational-map-extension from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety) directly to upgrade the symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular map \(J \to A\), and the would-be Picard-functor / autoduality detour \(J \cong J^\vee\) routes through the theorem of the cube, excised from this project iter-163. Sub-step~A.4 therefore inherits the Theorem~3.2 + Lemma~3.3 codim-\(1\) extension + Weil-divisor API + Auslander--Buchsbaum sub-build (the last absent from Mathlib). (See iter-172 audit at \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP}, NOTE block + \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} proof.) (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2500{+}\) LOC, \(\sim 22\)--\(35\) iters.)
286	  \end{itemize}

1013	[Sheafification inverts a localizer morphism of presheaves of modules]
1014	  \label{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}
1015	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isIso_sheafification_map_of_W}
1016	  Let \(R_0\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a
1017	  Grothendieck topology \(J\), let \(R_{sh}\) be its sheafification, and let
1018	  \(\alpha : R_0 \to R_{sh}.\mathrm{obj}\) be the (locally bijective) comparison
1019	  map of presheaves of rings. Let \(f : A \to B\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1020	  \(R_0\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1021	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\) lies in the sheafification
1022	  localizer class \(J.W\) --- equivalently, is locally bijective for \(J\). Then
1023	  the presheaf-of-modules sheafification functor
1024	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\,\alpha\) sends \(f\) to an
1025	  isomorphism:
1026	  \[
1027	    (\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f
1028	    \quad\text{is an isomorphism.}
1029	  \]
1030	  This is a closed go/no-go bridge --- it converts ``\,a morphism lies in
1031	  \(J.W\)\,'' into ``\,its sheafification is invertible\,''. Under route~(e) the
1032	  associator is the API-derived one (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), so this bridge is

1042	  \(J.W.\mathtt{inverseImage}\,(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0)\) of morphisms whose
1043	  underlying map of presheaves of abelian groups is locally bijective. Concretely,
1044	  the underlying \(\mathtt{AddCommGrp}\)-valued sheafification inverts exactly the

1047	  isomorphisms}\) identifies the preimage of \(J.W\) under

1052	  that preimage, hence \((\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f\) is an
1053	  isomorphism. No new construction is required beyond instantiating the existing
1054	  Mathlib identity at one morphism; this lemma is a closed thin wrapper, retained

1185	
1186	\begin{lemma}
1187	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1188	
1189	\leanok
1190	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1191	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1192	  % \lean: this superseded, off-critical-path obligation is the SAME statement
1193	  % (left-whiskering of a J.W-morphism by an arbitrary F is locally injective) as
1194	  % the LIVE lemma lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk, and is realised by
1195	  % the same sorry-free decl. Both blueprint nodes legitimately share the pin
1196	  % (leandag flags only Lean-side name collisions, not shared blueprint pins).
1197	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W}
1198	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1199	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1200	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1201	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1202	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1203	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1204	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1205	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1206	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1207	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1208	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1209	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1210	  left-whiskered morphism
1211	  \[
1212	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1213	  \]
1214	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1215	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1216	
1217	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1218	  \emph{full} instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the
1219	  load-bearing field of the off-path \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal-category
1220	  path (arbitrary \(F\)). Its content for an arbitrary \(F\) is exactly the live lemma
1221	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, which the associator's
1222	  sheafification transport consumes and which is closed \emph{unconditionally} by the
1223	  d.2 stalk--tensor commutation (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); the d.1/d.2
1224	  ingredients are therefore on the critical path. The two proofs recorded below --- a
1225	  sheaf-level argument specialised to locally trivial \(F\), and the general stalkwise
1226	  argument via (d.1)/(d.2) --- are retained for the historical record; the live group
1227	  law closes this obligation in its general (d.2) form through
1228	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1229	\end{lemma}
1230	
1231	\begin{proof}
1232	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1233	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1234	
1235	
1236	\leanok
1237	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1238	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1239	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1240	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1241	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1242	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1243	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1244	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1245	  On such a \(V\):
1246	  \begin{itemize}
1247	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1248	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1249	      \[
1250	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1251	      \]
1252	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1253	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1254	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1255	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1256	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1257	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1258	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1259	  \end{itemize}
1260	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1261	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1262	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1263	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1264	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
